result.markdown:
--- title: "Who's Actually Building Cycling Infrastructure?" subtitle: "Access to high-comfort bike networks across Canadian cities, 2019–2024" author: "Sam Lumley" date: "2026-04-20" format: html: theme: flatly --- ::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"} This is preliminary work based on openly available data. It is not an official release and should not be cited as such. ✌️ ::: ## 🚲 Intro Bike infrastructure is one of the cheapest ways a city can expand who can get around — but only one kind actually works. Painted lanes on busy roads do almost nothing for ridership — [the research on this is settled](URL_TODO). What moves the needle is **high-comfort** infrastructure: physically separated cycle tracks, multi-use paths, and local street bikeways. Places where you don't have to negotiate with a moving car. Between 2019 and 2024, Canadian cities added thousands of kilometres of cycling infrastructure. Not all of it was equal. Not all cities performed equally. Using two releases of the **[Canadian Cycling Network Database (CCND)](URL_TODO)** and the **[Can-BICS](URL_TODO)** classification framework, we measured how access to high-comfort infrastructure changed across three cities: Victoria, Montreal, and Toronto. **TL;DR:** Victoria punches way above its weight. Montreal has made real gains. Toronto has barely moved — despite being Canada's largest city, and despite cycling investment being perpetually promised and perpetually deferred. --- ## 📊 Data The analysis uses two national snapshots of Canadian cycling infrastructure: - **[2019 release](https://health-infobase.canada.ca/datalab/bicycling-infrastructure.html)** — baseline network, published by the Public Health Agency of Canada - **[2024 release](URL_TODO)** — five years of additions Each dataset is a collection of line segments — one per stretch of infrastructure — with attributes describing the type of facility. ::: {#tbl-raw-counts .cell tbl-cap='Raw segment counts and total kilometres by city and year.' execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} # TODO: replace with real data once processed # Expected columns: city, year, n_segments, total_km, high_comfort_km summary = pd.DataFrame({ "City": ["Victoria", "Victoria", "Montreal", "Montreal", "Toronto", "Toronto"], "Year": [2019, 2024, 2019, 2024, 2019, 2024], "Total km": [None, None, None, None, None, None], "High-comfort km": [None, None, None, None, None, None], "% High-comfort": [None, None, None, None, None, None], }) # Uncomment when data is ready: # summary = pd.read_csv(DATA / "network_summary.csv") summary.style \ .format({"Total km": "{:.0f}", "High-comfort km": "{:.0f}", "% High-comfort": "{:.1f}%"}, na_rep="—") \ .set_properties(**{"text-align": "right"}) \ .set_table_styles([{"selector": "th", "props": [("text-align", "left")]}]) \ .hide(axis="index") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=56} ```{=html}
City Year Total km High-comfort km % High-comfort
Victoria 2019 — — —
Victoria 2024 — — —
Montreal 2019 — — —
Montreal 2024 — — —
Toronto 2019 — — —
Toronto 2024 — — —
``` ::: ::: --- ## 🏷️ Classification is hard Here's a problem that doesn't get enough attention in cycling research: municipalities have wildly inconsistent names for their infrastructure. One city's "bike lane" is a painted stripe in the door zone. Another's is a fully protected cycle track with a physical barrier. A third calls the same thing a "multi-use path," a "cycling corridor," or something that doesn't map cleanly to any standard category at all. This matters because the raw data reflects whatever label each municipality chose to use — not a consistent, objective description of what was actually built. ### How we resolved it We used **[Can-BICS](URL_TODO)** (Canadian Bikeway Comfort and Safety) as a common framework. Can-BICS classifies cycling infrastructure into three comfort tiers based on physical separation from motor traffic: *high*, *medium*, and *low*. This analysis focuses on the high-comfort tier — the facilities with genuine physical separation that [research shows actually shift ridership](URL_TODO). To apply Can-BICS consistently, we verified infrastructure classifications using **Google Street View**, checking a sample of segments in each city where the attribute data was ambiguous or inconsistent. ::: {#cell-fig-gsv-example .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig_path = FIGS / "gsv_example.png" if fig_path.exists(): import matplotlib.image as mpimg img = mpimg.imread(fig_path) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5)) ax.imshow(img) ax.axis("off") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() else: fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5)) ax.text(0.5, 0.5, "Street View verification example\n(add screenshot to figures/gsv_example.png)", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=13, color="#888", transform=ax.transAxes) ax.axis("off") ax.set_title("Classification verification — placeholder", color="#aaa") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Example of Street View verification: the same infrastructure type labelled differently across municipalities. TODO: replace with actual Street View screenshot.](index_files/figure-html/fig-gsv-example-output-1.png){#fig-gsv-example width=1335 height=735} ::: ::: --- ## 📍 How do we measure spatial access? We measure access at the **dissemination block (DB)** level — Statistics Canada's smallest geographic unit with population counts. There are thousands of them per city. For each one, we ask: is there high-comfort infrastructure nearby? ::: {#cell-fig-method-diagram .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5)) # Draw a stylised block grid for x in range(5): for y in range(4): rect = plt.Rectangle((x, y), 0.9, 0.9, linewidth=0.5, edgecolor="#bbb", facecolor="#f5f5f5") ax.add_patch(rect) # A high-comfort segment running diagonally ax.plot([0.5, 4.5], [0.5, 3.5], color="#2196F3", linewidth=3, label="High-comfort segment", zorder=3) # Show two centroids and their buffers centroids = [(0.45, 2.45), (2.45, 0.45)] colours = ["#4CAF50", "#FF9800"] labels = ["Within 300 m → access = 1", "Outside 300 m → access = 0"] for (cx, cy), colour, label in zip(centroids, colours, labels): circle = plt.Circle((cx, cy), 0.8, color=colour, alpha=0.2) ax.add_patch(circle) ax.plot(cx, cy, "o", color=colour, markersize=7, zorder=4) ax.annotate(label, (cx, cy), xytext=(cx + 0.15, cy + 0.5), fontsize=9, color=colour, arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color=colour, lw=0.8)) ax.set_xlim(-0.2, 5.2) ax.set_ylim(-0.2, 4.5) ax.set_aspect("equal") ax.axis("off") ax.set_title("300 m buffer access method", fontsize=12, pad=10) blue_line = mpatches.Patch(color="#2196F3", label="High-comfort segment") ax.legend(handles=[blue_line], loc="upper left", frameon=False, fontsize=9) plt.tight_layout() plt.savefig(FIGS / "method_diagram.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight") plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Each dissemination block centroid is buffered by 300 m (straight-line distance). A block has access if any high-comfort segment falls within that buffer.](index_files/figure-html/fig-method-diagram-output-1.png){#fig-method-diagram width=794 height=735} ::: ::: For each DB centroid $i$, we compute a binary access indicator: $$ a_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } d(i,\ \text{nearest high-comfort segment}) \leq 300\text{ m} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ Then roll it up to a city-level **population-weighted access share**: $$ A = \frac{\sum_i p_i \cdot a_i}{\sum_i p_i} $$ where $p_i$ is the 2021 census population of block $i$. Dense blocks count more. A gain in a high-rise neighbourhood outweighs the same gain in a low-density suburb. That's the point. ### Why 300 m? 300 m is roughly a 4-minute walk, and a [well-established threshold](URL_TODO) in transport geography for "close enough to use." We also tested 200 m and 500 m to confirm the story doesn't change with the threshold. It doesn't — city rankings are consistent across all three distances. --- ### 🏆 Victoria: the standout Victoria is the smallest city in this analysis. It also has the most dramatic trajectory. Between 2019 and 2024, the share of Victoria residents within 300 m of high-comfort infrastructure rose from **[X]% to [Y]%** — a gain of **[Z] percentage points**. Per capita, nothing else here comes close. What drove this? Victoria built protected lanes on its core arterials — routes that cut through high-density residential areas and connect directly to downtown. When you build in the right place, a small investment reaches a lot of people. ::: {#cell-fig-victoria-map .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} import matplotlib.image as mpimg fig_path = FIGS / "victoria_map.png" if fig_path.exists(): img = mpimg.imread(fig_path) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7)) ax.imshow(img) ax.axis("off") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() else: fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7)) ax.text(0.5, 0.5, "Map will appear here after running\nscripts/03_make_figures.py", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=13, color="#888", transform=ax.transAxes) ax.axis("off") ax.set_title("Victoria — network map placeholder", color="#aaa") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Victoria cycling network, 2019 (grey) and new high-comfort segments added by 2024 (blue). Dissemination block centroids shaded by access status in 2024.](index_files/figure-html/fig-victoria-map-output-1.png){#fig-victoria-map width=1335 height=1035} ::: ::: Victoria's compact size is part of the story. A modest number of route-kilometres, placed well, can cover a substantial share of the city. That's not a caveat — the political will to build here was real, and hard-won. But it does mean the challenge scales differently for bigger cities. --- ### 🥯 Montreal: real gains, real network Montreal has the largest absolute high-comfort network of the three cities. And it kept growing. Between 2019 and 2024, population-weighted access rose from **[X]% to [Y]%**. In raw kilometres, Montreal's gains dwarf Victoria's. Central to this: the **REV** — Réseau Express Vélo. A set of high-frequency protected lanes built explicitly as cycling rapid transit, not recreational paths. That framing matters. ::: {#cell-fig-montreal-map .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig_path = FIGS / "montreal_map.png" if fig_path.exists(): img = mpimg.imread(fig_path) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7)) ax.imshow(img) ax.axis("off") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() else: fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7)) ax.text(0.5, 0.5, "Map will appear here after running\nscripts/03_make_figures.py", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=13, color="#888", transform=ax.transAxes) ax.axis("off") ax.set_title("Montreal — network map placeholder", color="#aaa") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Montreal cycling network, 2019 (grey) and new high-comfort segments added by 2024 (green). REV corridors visible as high-density additions in central arrondissements.](index_files/figure-html/fig-montreal-map-output-1.png){#fig-montreal-map width=1335 height=1035} ::: ::: The catch: Montreal is large and uneven. Gains have concentrated in the central plateau and downtown. Outer arrondissements have seen much less. The city-wide number is real, but it masks a lot of geography. --- ### 🚧 Toronto: a city marking time Toronto is Canada's largest city. It's also, by this measure, one of its most underbuilt. Between 2019 and 2024, population-weighted access moved from **[X]% to [Y]%** — roughly **[Z] percentage points** for a city of 2.7 million people. ::: {#cell-fig-toronto-map .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig_path = FIGS / "toronto_map.png" if fig_path.exists(): img = mpimg.imread(fig_path) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7)) ax.imshow(img) ax.axis("off") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() else: fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7)) ax.text(0.5, 0.5, "Map will appear here after running\nscripts/03_make_figures.py", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=13, color="#888", transform=ax.transAxes) ax.axis("off") ax.set_title("Toronto — network map placeholder", color="#aaa") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Toronto cycling network, 2019 (grey) and new high-comfort segments added by 2024 (orange). Most new additions are concentrated in the inner city; the inner suburbs remain largely unserved.](index_files/figure-html/fig-toronto-map-output-1.png){#fig-toronto-map width=1335 height=1035} ::: ::: This isn't a budget story. Toronto's capital allocation for cycling grew during this period. The city has an adopted cycling network plan. The gap is between plan and delivery. Protected lanes get announced, deferred, scoped down, or killed outright following political opposition. The data is a cumulative record of those decisions. --- ### The divergence ::: {#cell-fig-divergence .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} # TODO: replace with actual values once access results are computed # results = pd.read_csv(DATA / "access_results.csv") fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 5)) ax.text(0.5, 0.5, "Populate with real data\n(scripts/02_compute_access.py)", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=13, color="#888", transform=ax.transAxes) ax.axis("off") ax.set_title("The gap is widening — placeholder", color="#aaa") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Population-weighted access to high-comfort cycling infrastructure, 2019 and 2024, by city. Lines connect the same city across years.](index_files/figure-html/fig-divergence-output-1.png){#fig-divergence width=885 height=735} ::: ::: | City | 2019 | 2024 | Change | |------|------|------|--------| | Victoria | —% | —% | +— pp | | Montreal | —% | —% | +— pp | | Toronto | —% | —% | +— pp | : Population-weighted access to high-comfort cycling infrastructure. Percentage of residents within 300 m of a high-comfort segment. {#tbl-results} --- ## 🔍 Conclusion This isn't a story about resources or city size. It's about political choices. Victoria and Montreal built the kind of infrastructure the evidence says works. Toronto didn't. That matters beyond cycling mode share. High-comfort networks are a transit multiplier: - 🚌 **They extend the walkshed** around transit stops — critical for first/last mile in medium-density areas where bus frequency alone can't close the gap - 📉 **They reduce peak-hour vehicle pressure** on commute corridors - 🙋 **They reach new riders** — people who won't ride on painted lanes but will ride on protected ones. That's the mechanism behind modal shift. The CCND will be updated again. When it is, the question is whether the gap between cities like Victoria and Montreal and cities like Toronto has kept widening — or whether the laggards have finally started catching up. Based on what this analysis shows, there's no reason to expect catch-up without a real change in what actually gets built. --- ## Appendix: data and code All analysis code is in the `scripts/` directory. 1. `scripts/01_load_data.py` — load and classify CCND shapefiles 2. `scripts/02_compute_access.py` — compute DB-level access indicators 3. `scripts/03_make_figures.py` — generate maps and export figures ::: {#tbl-session-info .cell tbl-cap='Python package versions used in this analysis.' execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true"} import importlib.metadata pkgs = ["geopandas", "pandas", "matplotlib", "contextily", "shapely", "numpy"] rows = [] for pkg in pkgs: try: rows.append({"Package": pkg, "Version": importlib.metadata.version(pkg)}) except importlib.metadata.PackageNotFoundError: rows.append({"Package": pkg, "Version": "not installed"}) pd.DataFrame(rows).style.hide(axis="index") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=63} ```{=html}
Package Version
geopandas 1.1.3
pandas 3.0.2
matplotlib 3.10.8
contextily 1.7.0
shapely 2.1.2
numpy 2.4.4
``` ::: :::
